news_summary: Innovative restoration efforts and scientific advances are fostering hope for environmental resilience, with community-led rebuilding in Gaza, progress in predicting Antarctic sea-level changes, and initiatives reviving endangered native trees, despite challenges like climate-driven coastal erosion in Kenya.
news_summary_fr: Des initiatives innovantes en matière de restauration et les progrès scientifiques suscitent l'espoir d'une résilience environnementale, grâce notamment à la reconstruction menée par les communautés à Gaza, aux avancées dans la prévision des variations du niveau de la mer en Antarctique et aux initiatives visant à redonner vie à des essences indigènes menacées, et ce malgré des défis tels que l'érosion côtière liée au changement climatique au Kenya.
news_summary_es: Las innovadoras iniciativas de restauración y los avances científicos están alimentando la esperanza en la resiliencia medioambiental, gracias a la reconstrucción impulsada por la comunidad en Gaza, los avances en la predicción de los cambios del nivel del mar en la Antártida y las iniciativas para recuperar especies de árboles autóctonos en peligro de extinción, a pesar de retos como la erosión costera provocada por el cambio climático en Kenia.

articles.title: Podcast Transcript June 19th, 2026— (Guest Interview) Hope can rise: two sisters in Gaza turning rubble into building blocks | Earth Prize 2026
articles.summary:
Episode Description: Tala and Farah Mousa received thousands of messages after they won the Earth Prize. “You gave us hope we had completely lost,” people wrote. “You showed us we can be seen.” They were sheltering in a tent when those messages came in. This episode is about what it takes to build something: a […]
The post Podcast Transcript June 19th, 2026— (Guest Interview) Hope can rise: two sisters in Gaza turning rubble into building blocks | Earth Prize 2026 first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
Episode Description:

Tala and Farah Mousa received thousands of messages after they won the Earth Prize. “You gave us hope we had completely lost,” people wrote. “You showed us we can be seen.” They were sheltering in a tent when those messages came in.

This episode is about what it takes to build something: a real, physical method for turning rubble into building blocks, while living inside the rubble. Arielle speaks to the teenaged sisters from Gaza about the idea that came from a textbook the morning after their home was bombed, the competition that brought their work to the world, and what they want for their community, their generation, and the ones who come after them.

Connect with Build Hope Palestine:

Follow Build Hope Palestine on Instagram: @build.hope

The Earth Prize website: theearthprize.org

Further reading:

The Optimist Daily articles on Earth Prize 2026 regional winners Part I and Part II

Learn about the Earth Prize 2026 global winner

If you have questions, comments, feedback, suggestions, or just want to say hi, send a message to: podcast@optimistdaily.com.

Want to be part of the Optimism Movement? Become an Emissary.

Subscribe to our FREE Daily/Weekly Newsletter and follow us on Instagram, X, and Blue Sky.

The Optimist Daily is a project of the World Business Academy.

Donate link: https://www.optimistdaily.com/donate-to-support-the-optimist-daily/?gift=Y%20http://

Theme and all original music by Marvin Lanes

Transcript:

Tala:

Ones who can’t speak. I want to be their voice. I want to work for human rights and human dignity.

Arielle (Voice Over):

61 million tons of rubble. That number is so large, so enormous, it stops feeling like a number at all. It’s 169 kilograms of broken buildings for every square meter of the Gaza Strip. At the time of this recording, more than 80% of all structures there have been damaged or destroyed. The UN puts the reconstruction bill at around $70 billion, and says rebuilding could take decades.

Rubble is what remains when a home is bombed. It is the material of loss. Welcome to The Optimist Daily’s Weekly Roundup. It’s Arielle here, back with another guest interview with two very special, resilient teens, who I am sure are going to inspire you just as much as they have inspired me.

Tala:

Hi, I’m Tala. I’m Palestinian girl from Gaza.

Farah:

And I’m Farah. I’m also a Palestinian girl from Gaza.

Arielle (Voice Over):

Tala and Farah Mousa were 16 and 14 years old respectively when their home was bombed. Before the devastation hit, their family evacuated in a rush. Tala grabbed a bag and tried to fill it with whatever was in front of her. The last thing she threw in was her technology textbook.

The day after the bombing, still shocked and devastated by the destruction of her home, Tala flipped open the book. There was a lesson about making blocks from mountain stone. She looked at the rubble all around her, the physical jagged fragments of broken lives, communities, and homes, and thought, “What if you didn’t clear it away? What if this rubble was part of the answer?”

Today, Tala is 17 and Farah is 15. Together they built Build Hope Palestine, a method for turning rubble into reusable construction blocks. 80% recycled rubble, mixed with clay and ash and glass powder. Strong enough for partitions, pavement, shelters, tent foundations— designed so anyone who learns how to do it can teach it to someone else. The Earth Prize is the world’s largest environmental competition for teenagers. 15,000 entrants from 160 countries. One winner per region. This year, the Middle East winner was Build Hope Palestine, and the two sisters who dreamed it up while sheltering in a tent in Gaza.

They are the first Palestinian team to have won it. I had the privilege of speaking to them about their accomplishments, what keeps them motivated these days, and about who they are as people. They were still in their tent in the middle Gaza region while on the call with me, so you may hear some noise in the background here and there.

You’ll hear Tala’s voice first, followed by her sister, Farah.

Arielle:

Can you tell us about who you both were before you started this Build Hope mission?

Tala:

We were, like, just two simple sisters from Gaza. We love studying, we love, uh, volunteering and engaging and participating in community activities. And somehow we had the vision that we will make something in the future, especially for the youth in Gaza.

I always dreamed to, to study international law and maybe to be an ambassador for Palestine one day. ‘Cause I always want to be the voice of children and women here in Gaza, the one who can’t speak. I want to be their voice. I want to work for human rights and human dignity.

Farah:

Uh, I want to study translation and language because I want to share our story in different, uh, lang- language, and yeah.

Arielle (Voice Over):

Pretty big and noble aspirations, right? I asked them where these dreams came from for them.

Tala:

It all about our childhood. Like, our childhood wasn’t something really normal. It wasn’t like the normal that any child in the world have, ’cause we faced multiple wars, not the only one which was the last one.

Every one of them affect us, like affect our vision, affect our minds, affect the way how we think and how we want to be in the future. So it was the biggest motivator for us. I want maybe to work in international law ’cause I want to defend my people. Farah want to share our story. So it’s all, it’s all about our generation.

It’s all about our city. It’s all about Gaza.

Arielle (Voice Over):

And what is it that they are fighting for? What do they want for their generation and for the generations of children that come after them?

Tala:

Just live in peace. They don’t need something complicated. They just want to live a normal life like anyone or any children can live in the world.

They want to live in peace, go to school, and have a, a balanced, uh, food maybe. We are talking about basic human needs. We’re not talking about something complex.

Arielle (Voice Over):

Tala and Farah already had huge ambitions for their futures and for the futures of their peers. But the details of Build Hope hadn’t been solidified yet.

I asked them when the idea of Build Hope Palestine really came to life.

Arielle:

Can you take me back to the moment where the idea for Build Hope clicked for you?

Tala:

Maybe you are now waiting for a happy moment, but the reality is away from that. When our home was bombed, it was a day full of cries and tears. It was really sadness everywhere.

But we don’t have wardrobes because we live in a tent, and in the tent there is no space. We only have a bag. And that bag, I hold it from the last time we were in our home before it was bombed. So I put anything I saw in it. The final thing, it was my technology book It was the day after our home was bombed.

I want to, to do something that made me attached somehow to my home, satisfied my belonging feeling, satisfied my, my, my inner voice here who want to, to do anything to be attached to, to her, to her home. So when I read the book, there was a lesson about how we make blocks in usual, like how they, uh, make stones and blocks from the mountains.

There is, uh, how to make stones, and there is the news of yesterday that our home was bombed literally. So when everything was in one mixture, the idea literally clicked. What if we replaced the mountain stone with rubble? What if we make a reconstruction? What, what if and what if? And anything, uh, the first one who I shared my ideas with is Farah.

So I told her, and she loved the idea. She loved it, and she supported me. We just have a deep discussion after that, and then we go straight to our mom, and we told her, because she is an industrial engineer, so she have an idea about how they do these things.

Arielle (Voice Over):

What Tala and Farah built was designed to run without them.

Anyone who learns the method can teach it to someone else, so the knowledge keeps moving no matter what. So how are the blocks made anyway? We asked Farah to explain the process.

Farah:

Okay. First, we collect rubble. We collect the safe rubble and crush it, mix it with local binders such as clay, ash, and, uh, glass powder, and we mix it, uh, together with, um, water, and we put it in the model for seven, seven days?

Yeah.

Tala:

Yeah. Two days in the model and five without it.

Farah:

Yeah. Uh-

Tala: And it will be ready for use, for non-load bearing use.

Farah:

For non, non-load bearing. Yeah. And non-load bearing is like, it’s, um, for partitions, pavement, for temporary shelters, for, um, what else? Settlement?

Tala:

Yeah, for settling the, the- Tent … tent.

Arielle (Voice Over):

Solutions that are self-sustaining, that don’t need the architect of the original solution to keep the positive impact going, are some of the best kinds of solutions out there It’s so impressive that Tala and Farah were able to come up with such a practical and immediate way to resolve the immense destruction facing them and their community.

Tala and Farah made it incredibly clear how they feel about their community, and how their love for and faith in those around them really fuel how they approach the world.

Tala:

People around us have passions, talent, and power. They are really creative. They are just waiting for the real chance. They are just waiting to be heard and to be seen from the world.

So I can tell you that from the day they announced us as the Middle East region- regional winner, me and Farah received thousands of message in our mobiles that, “You were our inspiration. You inspired us. You gave us hope, which we totally lost in the war. And you give us, like, a, a real experience that people in Gaza here can be seen from the world.”

Arielle:

Mm-hmm. And you both said that you feel like you’re representing basically all of the youth in Gaza right now, not just yourselves. How does that feel? Is that a lot of pressure, or do you find that very motivating?

Tala:

It- … was a bit pressure for us, uh, ’cause we are, like, a high schooler. How, how do you tell us, like, we are representing a whole country here and a whole generation of youth?

But after that, we get used it, and we loved it, to be, to be honest with you. We believe it’s, like, an honor for us to represent, uh, millions of youth here in Gaza, and also to give them a real experience that they can do it, but they need to keep going

Arielle (Voice Over):

After their regional Earth Prize win, messages came in from across Gaza saying things like, “You gave us hope we had lost. You showed us we can be seen.”

Carrying that at such young ages, in a tent, in an active conflict zone, as Tala expressed, it’s an honor and it’s also a real weight. I asked them what gets them through on days when their work feels too heavy to continue.

Tala:

Basically, when we don’t want to give up, we just remember the first day the idea clicked our mind.

It was a day after home was bombed. We remember how that scene was. It was really bad, but somehow we made it a source of motivation and hope for us.

Arielle (Voice Over):

That day, when Build Hope Palestine was born out of the broken pieces of Tala and Farah’s home, eventually led up to the moment they won the regional Earth Prize for the Middle East.

The girls take us back to that moment.

Tala:

We were sitting next to each other. Yeah. Um, I was studying. The, she had her mobile in her hand, and then, uh, there was, like, a- an- an ala- an alert that, uh, from the Earth Prize there is an email. So she told me, like, “Tala, there is an email from the Earth Prize.” So I just, like, throw my books away.

And had, like, the, the mobile in our hands like this. We, we were literally shaking. And then when we open the email, um, we see a, a huge congratulation. So we believed, like, ah. It was a hard thing, and we finally made it. We did it. We nailed it. We start laughing, laughing in a crazy way. And then my mom, uh, came from our laughing sound, and she said, “What’s happened?

What? Did you, did you win?” We told her, yeah, and, uh, we start hugging each other and we start crying, and it was, like, the happiness cr- tears

Arielle (Voice Over):

Winning the Middle East Regional Earth Prize is already such a huge accomplishment, but Tala and Farah don’t see it as the end goal. They see it as a launchpad to further their positive impact. So what’s next for them?

Arielle:

And what comes next?

Tala:

For the project, we intend, uh, to make workshops next month, maybe when we have the, the money. ‘Cause we will launch workshops, train young people, and produ- produce blocks with them. ‘Cause we are not just intending them to, to learn how to make blocks, but no, we are giving them the awareness.

We are giving them the knowledge. We want them to share it with their peers, with their families, and their friends. But personally, um, I will be, as I told you, in my final year in high school in two month, so it will be a really hard year for me. And I will start applying for universities, uh, abroad, ’cause as you know, there’s no universities here in Gaza.

Uh, and Farah will be in her 10th grade, which also a hard grade, I, I, I, I think. And yeah, we will just keep going and always have the vision and the hope that we will make something, something for this country.

Arielle (Voice Over):

To keep up with Tala and Farah’s progress and to support Build Hope Palestine, make sure to follow their Instagram, which is @build.hope.

Don’t worry, I will link this in the show notes. You can also read all about the other fantastic regional winners in a piece we wrote about them recently, which will also be linked, along with the Earth Prize website so you can learn all about their amazing work and check out the team who ended up winning the global prize Tala and Farah were such a delight to speak with.

I honestly feel so privileged to have had the opportunity to meet such wonderful, willful young women, and I can’t wait to share with you what the best advice they had ever received was, and on top of that, some words of wisdom of their own. But before we do that, I just want to thank all you optimists out there simply for being here, for believing that we can make this world a better place.

If our solutions-focused mission resonates with you, then please share our work with your friends and family or anyone that you feel needs a bit more positivity in their lives. Hit that follow button so that you don’t miss an episode, subscribe to our free daily or weekly newsletter, follow us on socials, @optimistdaily on pretty much everything except for X, where we are @odetooptimism.

Every little thing, every little like, subscribe, follow, or positive rating really does support us and bolster us. It keeps us motivated to continue doing this work. On that note, we want to give an extra special thank you to our emissaries who are our financial supporters. If you want to join in and become an Emissary with a one-time, monthly, or annual donation, there is a link for that in the show notes.

Emissaries also get a perk where they can shout out a person, organization, charity, whatever and whomever is generating good in their world. And Karissa and I will either read their message aloud here, or Emissaries can leave a voice note that we will play on the podcast. If you’re already an Emissary, you should have a link for that in your email.

And if you become one, then you’ll get one shortly after signing up. All right. With all that said and done, I’m turning it over to Tala and Farah to share the best advice they’ve ever received.

Tala:

My mom once told us, “Never let your circumstances decide the, the size of your dreams.” The time I heard it, I was, like, uh, fourteen years old.

I didn’t understand it, but now I am living with it. This advice, uh, the, the, the… my biggest source of motivation, not only me, like Farah also, ’cause we had hard circumstances. But look at us now, like, we didn’t make these circumstances decide the size of our dreams, literally.

Arielle (Voice Over):

And when I asked if they had anything else that they wanted to leave us with, here’s Tala.

Tala:

It would be only that hope can rise. Hope can rise amid destruction. Hope can rise, uh, over rubble and darkness. Hope can rise. Just have a faith in your heart and, uh, a plan in your mind, and always work on it, and I can tell you, hope can rise.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes a tangible, impactful innovation by two young sisters in Gaza who developed a method to recycle rubble from war destruction into reusable building blocks. This solution addresses a massive environmental and humanitarian challenge with potential broad benefits for rebuilding communities and inspiring hope in a conflict zone. The story is detailed, focused, and highlights significant positive real-world impact and human resilience.
articles.personality_title: Two sisters in Gaza turn war rubble into building blocks to rebuild their community
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gaza has faced heavy destruction, with more than 80% of buildings damaged or destroyed. Rebuilding is costly and could take decades. Amid this, two teenage sisters, Tala and Farah Mousa, came up with a creative way to help their community using the rubble left by bombings.

**What happened** – After their home was bombed, Tala, 17, found inspiration in a textbook lesson about making stone blocks. She and her sister Farah, 15, created Build Hope Palestine, a method to recycle rubble by mixing it with clay, ash, and glass powder to make strong building blocks. These blocks can be used to build walls, pavements, shelters, and tent foundations. Their project won the Middle East regional Earth Prize in 2026, a competition with 15,000 teen participants from 160 countries.

**Impact** – Their idea turns destruction into a resource, offering a practical and affordable way to rebuild while helping the environment by recycling debris. It also gives people in Gaza a way to actively participate in reconstruction, spreading knowledge through workshops. Their win brought thousands of messages from people who felt hope and recognition through the sisters’ work.

**What's next step** – Tala and Farah plan to hold workshops to teach young people how to make the blocks and share the knowledge widely. They also aim to continue their education abroad and keep working toward a better future for Gaza. Their project is designed so anyone who learns it can teach others, ensuring the idea grows beyond them.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Two young sisters in Gaza are turning war rubble into building blocks, creating a hopeful and practical step toward rebuilding their community from the ground up.
articles.personality_title_fr: Deux sœurs à Gaza transforment les décombres de guerre en briques pour reconstruire leur communauté
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Gaza a subi de lourdes destructions, plus de 80 % des bâtiments étant endommagés ou détruits. La reconstruction est coûteuse et pourrait prendre des décennies. Dans ce contexte, deux adolescentes, Tala et Farah Mousa, ont imaginé une solution créative pour aider leur communauté en utilisant les débris laissés par les bombardements.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Après que leur maison a été bombardée, Tala, 17 ans, a trouvé l'inspiration dans une leçon de manuel sur la fabrication de blocs de pierre. Avec sa sœur Farah, 15 ans, elles ont créé Build Hope Palestine, une méthode pour recycler les décombres en les mélangeant avec de l'argile, de la cendre et de la poudre de verre afin de fabriquer des briques solides. Ces briques peuvent servir à construire des murs, des pavés, des abris et des fondations de tentes. Leur projet a remporté le prix régional Moyen-Orient de l'Earth Prize 2026, un concours rassemblant 15 000 adolescents de 160 pays.

**Impact** – Leur idée transforme la destruction en ressource, offrant une façon pratique et abordable de reconstruire tout en aidant l'environnement grâce au recyclage des débris. Elle permet aussi aux habitants de Gaza de participer activement à la reconstruction en diffusant ce savoir par des ateliers. Leur victoire a suscité des milliers de messages de personnes retrouvant espoir et reconnaissance grâce à leur travail.

**Prochaine étape** – Tala et Farah prévoient d'organiser des ateliers pour enseigner aux jeunes comment fabriquer ces briques et diffuser largement cette connaissance. Elles souhaitent aussi poursuivre leurs études à l'étranger et continuer à œuvrer pour un avenir meilleur à Gaza. Leur projet est conçu pour que toute personne formée puisse à son tour enseigner, assurant ainsi la pérennité de l'idée.

**Phrase clé** – Deux jeunes sœurs à Gaza transforment les décombres de guerre en briques, offrant une étape concrète et pleine d’espoir pour reconstruire leur communauté.
articles.personality_title_es: Dos hermanas en Gaza convierten escombros de guerra en bloques para reconstruir su comunidad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Gaza ha sufrido grandes destrucciones, con más del 80 % de sus edificios dañados o destruidos. La reconstrucción es costosa y podría tardar décadas. En medio de esto, dos hermanas adolescentes, Tala y Farah Mousa, idearon una forma creativa de ayudar a su comunidad usando los escombros dejados por los bombardeos.

**Qué pasó** – Después de que su casa fue bombardeada, Tala, de 17 años, encontró inspiración en una lección de un libro sobre cómo hacer bloques de piedra. Ella y su hermana Farah, de 15 años, crearon Build Hope Palestine, un método para reciclar escombros mezclándolos con arcilla, ceniza y polvo de vidrio para fabricar bloques de construcción resistentes. Estos bloques se pueden usar para hacer paredes, pavimentos, refugios y bases para tiendas de campaña. Su proyecto ganó el premio regional de Oriente Medio del Earth Prize 2026, una competencia con 15,000 jóvenes de 160 países.

**Impacto** – Su idea convierte la destrucción en un recurso, ofreciendo una forma práctica y económica de reconstruir, al mismo tiempo que ayuda al medio ambiente reciclando los escombros. También da a la gente de Gaza una manera de participar activamente en la reconstrucción, difundiendo el conocimiento a través de talleres. Su victoria trajo miles de mensajes de personas que encontraron esperanza y reconocimiento gracias al trabajo de las hermanas.

**Próximo paso** – Tala y Farah planean organizar talleres para enseñar a jóvenes cómo hacer los bloques y compartir el conocimiento ampliamente. También desean continuar sus estudios en el extranjero y seguir trabajando por un futuro mejor para Gaza. Su proyecto está diseñado para que cualquiera que aprenda pueda enseñar a otros, asegurando que la idea siga creciendo.

**Frase clave** – Dos jóvenes hermanas en Gaza están convirtiendo escombros de guerra en bloques de construcción, dando un paso práctico y esperanzador para reconstruir su comunidad desde sus cimientos.
articles.image_prompt: Two young girls’ silhouettes made of light stand united amid a softly glowing pile of rubble transformed into neatly stacked building blocks, with gentle hands shaping the blocks, all set against a warm, muted palette evoking hope and resilience in a fragile, shelter-like environment.

articles.title: Emergent decadal predictability in Antarctic contribution to sea-level rise
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 17 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10614-4Although Antarctic ice loss projections diverge widely by 2100, this Perspective shows that present-day rates robustly predict mid-century sea level rise, providing a firm basis for near-term planning, while highlighting priorities for model development aimed at resolving longer-term sea level rise uncertainty.
articles.content:
Noble, T. L. et al. The sensitivity of the Antarctic Ice Sheet to a changing climate: past, present, and future. Rev. Geophys. 58, e2019RG000663 (2020).

Cook, A. J. & Vaughan, D. G. Overview of areal changes of the ice shelves on the Antarctic Peninsula over the past 50 years. Cryosphere 4, 77–98 (2010).

Walker, C. C. et al. Multi-decadal collapse of East Antarctica’s Conger–Glenzer Ice Shelf. Nat. Geosci. 17, 1240–1248 (2024).

Fox-Kemper, B. et al. in Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis (eds Masson-Delmotte, V. et al.) Ch. 9 (Cambridge Univ. Press, 2021).

Oppenheimer, M. et al. in IPCC Special Report on the Ocean and Cryosphere in a Changing Climate (eds Pörtner, H.-O. et al.) 126 (Cambridge Univ. Press, 2019).

DeConto, R. M. et al. The Paris Climate Agreement and future sea-level rise from Antarctica. Nature 593, 83–89 (2021). This study applies the MICI mechanism to project Antarctic Ice Sheet contributions to sea-level rise under Paris Agreement emissions scenarios, providing high-end estimates that have become central to planning for worst-case outcomes.

Otosaka, I. N. et al. Mass balance of the Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets from 1992 to 2020. Earth Syst. Sci. Data 15, 1597–1616 (2023).

Weertman, J. Stability of the junction of an ice sheet and an ice shelf. J. Glaciol. 13, 3–11 (1974).

Nye, J. F. The response of glaciers and ice-sheets to seasonal and climatic changes. Proc. R. Soc. Lond. Math. Phys. Sci. 256, 559–584 (1960).

Pattyn, F. The paradigm shift in Antarctic ice sheet modelling. Nat. Commun. 9, 2728 (2018).

Robel, A. A., Roe, G. H. & Haseloff, M. Response of marine-terminating glaciers to forcing: time scales, sensitivities, instabilities, and stochastic dynamics. J. Geophys. Res. Earth Surf. 123, 2205–2227 (2018). This study establishes the theoretical basis for reservoir time as a fundamental timescale governing how marine-terminating glaciers respond to external forcing, a concept this paper demonstrates is a robust and emergent feature across ISMIP6 models.

Seroussi, H. et al. ISMIP6 Antarctica: a multi-model ensemble of the Antarctic ice sheet evolution over the 21st century. Cryosphere 14, 3033–3070 (2020).

Seroussi, H. et al. Insights into the vulnerability of Antarctic glaciers from the ISMIP6 ice sheet model ensemble and associated uncertainty. Cryosphere 17, 5197–5217 (2023).

Seroussi, H. et al. Evolution of the Antarctic Ice Sheet over the next three centuries from an ISMIP6 model ensemble. Earths Future 12, e2024EF004561 (2024). This study presents the ISMIP6 Antarctic model ensemble used as the primary dataset in this study, making it an essential reference for interpreting the results.

Coulon, V. et al. Disentangling the drivers of future Antarctic ice loss with a historically calibrated ice-sheet model. Cryosphere 18, 653–681 (2024).

Rignot, E., Velicogna, I., van den Broeke, M. R., Monaghan, A. & Lenaerts, J. Acceleration of the contribution of the Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets to sea level rise. Geophys. Res. Lett. 38, L05503 (2011).

Fricker, H. A. et al. Antarctica in 2025: drivers of deep uncertainty in projected ice loss. Science 387, 601–609 (2025).

Bassis, J. & Walker, C. C. Upper and lower limits on the stability of calving glaciers from the yield strength envelope of ice. Proc. R. Soc. A 468, 913–931 (2011).

Adusumilli, S., Fricker, H. A., Medley, B., Padman, L. & Siegfried, M. R. Interannual variations in meltwater input to the Southern Ocean from Antarctic ice shelves. Nat. Geosci. 13, 616–620 (2020).

Smith, B. et al. Pervasive ice sheet mass loss reflects competing ocean and atmosphere processes. Science 368, 1239–1242 (2020).

Badgeley, J. A., Morlighem, M. & Seroussi, H. Increased sea-level contribution from northwestern Greenland for models that reproduce observations. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 122, e2411904122 (2025).

Choi, Y., Petty, A., Felikson, D. & Poterjoy, J. Estimation of the state and parameters in ice sheet model using an ensemble Kalman filter and Observing System Simulation Experiments. Cryosphere 19, 5423–5444 (2025).

Goldberg, D. N. & Heimbach, P. Parameter and state estimation with a time-dependent adjoint marine ice sheet model. Cryosphere 7, 1659–1678 (2013).

Beckmann, J. et al. Disentangling uncertainty in ISMIP6 Antarctic sub-shelf melting and 2300 sea level rise projections. Preprint at EGUsphere https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-2025-4069 (2025).

Brondex, J., Gillet-Chaulet, F. & Gagliardini, O. Sensitivity of centennial mass loss projections of the Amundsen Basin to the friction law. Cryosphere 13, 177–195 (2019).

Sun, S. et al. Antarctic ice sheet response to sudden and sustained ice-shelf collapse (ABUMIP). J. Glaciol. 66, 891–904 (2020).

articles.reasoning: The article presents a significant scientific advancement showing that current Antarctic ice loss rates can robustly predict mid-century sea level rise, providing a reliable basis for near-term planning and climate adaptation. This has broad societal and environmental implications by improving the accuracy of sea-level rise projections, which is crucial for global climate resilience and policy-making. The article is focused on a single important topic with detailed scientific context and references.
articles.personality_title: Scientists can now better predict sea level rise from Antarctic ice loss by 2050
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Antarctic Ice Sheet is a huge mass of ice that slowly melts and adds water to the oceans. Predicting how much ice will melt and raise sea levels is very hard because different computer models show very different results for the future.

**What happened** – A new scientific study found that the current speed of ice loss in Antarctica can reliably predict how much sea levels will rise by the middle of this century, around 2050. Researchers used many ice sheet models and real data to show that present-day ice loss rates give a clear picture of near-future sea level changes.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it reduces uncertainty about sea level rise in the next few decades. Knowing that current ice melt rates are a good guide helps governments and communities plan better for flooding and coastal changes. It also points out where models need improvement to predict longer-term changes beyond 2050.

**What's next step** – Scientists will work on improving ice sheet models to better understand and predict ice loss after 2050. This will help create more accurate long-term sea level rise forecasts. Meanwhile, policymakers can use the current findings to make informed decisions about protecting coastal areas.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Present-day Antarctic ice loss rates give a trustworthy prediction of sea level rise by 2050, aiding near-term planning and climate adaptation efforts.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques peuvent désormais mieux prévoir la montée du niveau de la mer liée à la fonte de l’Antarctique d’ici 2050
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La calotte glaciaire de l’Antarctique est une énorme masse de glace qui fond lentement et ajoute de l’eau aux océans. Prédire la quantité de glace qui fondra et fera monter le niveau de la mer est difficile car différents modèles informatiques donnent des résultats très différents pour l’avenir.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle étude scientifique a montré que la vitesse actuelle de la fonte des glaces en Antarctique peut prédire de manière fiable la montée du niveau de la mer jusqu’au milieu de ce siècle, vers 2050. Les chercheurs ont utilisé plusieurs modèles de calottes glaciaires et des données réelles pour démontrer que les taux actuels de perte de glace donnent une image claire des changements à venir à court terme.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle réduit l’incertitude sur la montée du niveau de la mer dans les prochaines décennies. Savoir que les taux actuels de fonte sont un bon indicateur aide les gouvernements et les communautés à mieux se préparer aux inondations et aux changements côtiers. Cela montre aussi où les modèles doivent être améliorés pour mieux prévoir les changements après 2050.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont améliorer les modèles des calottes glaciaires pour mieux comprendre et prédire la fonte après 2050. Cela permettra d’avoir des prévisions plus précises à long terme. En attendant, les décideurs peuvent utiliser ces résultats pour mieux protéger les zones côtières.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les taux actuels de fonte des glaces en Antarctique fournissent une prévision fiable de la montée du niveau de la mer d’ici 2050, ce qui aide à la planification et à l’adaptation au changement climatique à court terme.
articles.personality_title_es: Los científicos pueden predecir mejor la subida del nivel del mar por la pérdida de hielo en la Antártida para 2050
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La capa de hielo de la Antártida es una gran masa de hielo que se derrite lentamente y añade agua a los océanos. Predecir cuánto hielo se derretirá y cuánto subirá el nivel del mar es difícil porque los modelos por computadora muestran resultados muy diferentes para el futuro.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio científico encontró que la velocidad actual de pérdida de hielo en la Antártida puede predecir con confianza cuánto subirá el nivel del mar hacia la mitad de este siglo, alrededor de 2050. Los investigadores usaron muchos modelos y datos reales para mostrar que las tasas actuales de pérdida de hielo dan una imagen clara de los cambios cercanos en el futuro.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque reduce la incertidumbre sobre la subida del nivel del mar en las próximas décadas. Saber que las tasas actuales de derretimiento son una buena guía ayuda a gobiernos y comunidades a planear mejor para inundaciones y cambios en las zonas costeras. También señala dónde se deben mejorar los modelos para predecir mejor los cambios a largo plazo después de 2050.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos trabajarán en mejorar los modelos de las capas de hielo para comprender y predecir mejor la pérdida de hielo después de 2050. Esto ayudará a crear pronósticos más precisos a largo plazo. Mientras tanto, los responsables políticos pueden usar este hallazgo para tomar decisiones informadas sobre protección costera.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las tasas actuales de pérdida de hielo en la Antártida ofrecen una predicción confiable de la subida del nivel del mar para 2050, apoyando la planificación a corto plazo y la adaptación climática.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, detailed painting of a vast Antarctic ice sheet gently melting into the calm ocean, with layered translucent ice shelves extending over the water and subtle, warm hues of dawn reflecting on the ice, symbolizing emerging predictability and hopeful progress in understanding sea-level rise.

articles.title: Revival project for endangered native trees
articles.summary: The population of black poplar trees has dwindled to around 7,000 trees in the UK and Ireland.
articles.content: Saplings which have been propagated from the few surviving trees in the county are being nurtured in Terrington, near Malton, before they are planted within the next two years.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a conservation effort to revive the endangered black poplar tree population, which has significant environmental and ecological benefits. The project involves propagating saplings from the remaining trees and replanting them, which is a meaningful action with positive impact on biodiversity and ecosystem restoration in the UK and Ireland. The story is focused and provides enough context about the initiative.
articles.personality_title: Project to revive endangered black poplar trees in UK and Ireland
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Black poplar trees are a rare type of tree in the UK and Ireland, with only about 7,000 left today. These trees are important for local environments but have been disappearing over time.

**What happened** – A conservation project is growing new black poplar saplings in Terrington, near Malton. These young trees come from the few remaining black poplars in the area. The plan is to plant these saplings back into the wild within the next two years.

**Impact** – This project helps increase the number of black poplar trees, which supports wildlife and improves natural habitats. By growing trees from local parents, the new trees are well suited to their environment, helping nature recover more effectively.

**What's next step** – The saplings will be carefully planted in different places over the next two years. This will help spread the trees and protect them from disappearing again.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A careful effort to grow and replant black poplar trees aims to restore an important and rare tree species in the UK and Ireland.
articles.personality_title_fr: Projet de restauration des peupliers noirs en danger au Royaume-Uni et en Irlande
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les peupliers noirs sont des arbres rares au Royaume-Uni et en Irlande, avec seulement environ 7 000 arbres restants. Ces arbres sont importants pour les environnements locaux, mais ont diminué avec le temps.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un projet de conservation fait pousser de jeunes peupliers noirs à Terrington, près de Malton. Ces jeunes arbres proviennent des quelques peupliers noirs encore présents dans la région. Le but est de replanter ces jeunes arbres dans la nature dans les deux prochaines années.

**Impact** – Ce projet aide à augmenter le nombre de peupliers noirs, ce qui soutient la faune et améliore les habitats naturels. En cultivant des arbres issus de parents locaux, les nouveaux arbres sont bien adaptés à leur environnement, aidant la nature à se rétablir plus efficacement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les jeunes arbres seront plantés avec soin dans différents endroits au cours des deux prochaines années. Cela aidera à étendre la présence des arbres et à les protéger de la disparition.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un effort attentif pour cultiver et replanter des peupliers noirs vise à restaurer une espèce d’arbre importante et rare au Royaume-Uni et en Irlande.
articles.personality_title_es: Proyecto para revivir los álamos negros en peligro en Reino Unido e Irlanda
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los álamos negros son árboles raros en Reino Unido e Irlanda, con solo alrededor de 7,000 árboles restantes. Estos árboles son importantes para los ambientes locales, pero han ido desapareciendo con el tiempo.

**Qué pasó** – Un proyecto de conservación está cultivando nuevos plantones de álamo negro en Terrington, cerca de Malton. Estos árboles jóvenes provienen de los pocos álamos negros que quedan en la zona. El plan es plantar estos plantones en la naturaleza durante los próximos dos años.

**Impacto** – Este proyecto ayuda a aumentar el número de álamos negros, lo que apoya la vida silvestre y mejora los hábitats naturales. Al cultivar árboles de padres locales, los nuevos árboles se adaptan bien a su entorno, ayudando a la naturaleza a recuperarse mejor.

**Próximo paso** – Los plantones serán plantados cuidadosamente en diferentes lugares durante los próximos dos años. Esto ayudará a extender los árboles y a protegerlos de desaparecer.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un esfuerzo cuidadoso para cultivar y replantar álamos negros busca restaurar una especie de árbol importante y rara en Reino Unido e Irlanda.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a small, vibrant nursery scene in a peaceful countryside setting near a village, showing tender young saplings carefully growing in handcrafted wooden containers surrounded by soft soil and gentle sunlight, with subtle silhouettes of ancient, large native trees in the distant background symbolizing their rare surviving ancestors.

articles.title: Kenya faces growing consequences of climate change-driven coastal erosion
articles.summary: Kenya just held a major conference on ocean protection, bringing together thousands of scientists, policymakers, and philanthropists. The goal is to find solutions to preserve marine resources and prevent environmental degradation. Over the past decade, the East African coast has been particularly affected by declining fish stocks, pollution, and erosion linked to rising sea levels. Bastien Renouil and Elodie Cousin met with communities affected by advancing waters, a problem that is becoming increasingly serious.
articles.content:
Kenya just held a major conference on ocean protection, bringing together thousands of scientists, policymakers, and philanthropists. The goal is to find solutions to preserve marine resources and prevent environmental degradation. Over the past decade, the East African coast has been particularly affected by declining fish stocks, pollution, and erosion linked to rising sea levels. Bastien Renouil and Elodie Cousin met with communities affected by advancing waters, a problem that is becoming increasingly serious.

Video by: Bastien RENOUIL
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a major conference in Kenya focused on ocean protection, involving thousands of scientists, policymakers, and philanthropists working to find solutions to climate change-driven coastal erosion and marine resource preservation. This represents a significant and positive real-world effort addressing broad environmental challenges with meaningful societal impact.
articles.personality_title: Kenya unites experts to protect its coast from climate change damage
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Kenya’s coast along the Indian Ocean has been facing serious problems like fewer fish, pollution, and land loss because of rising sea levels over the last ten years.

**What happened** – Recently, Kenya hosted a large conference on ocean protection. Thousands of scientists, government leaders, and donors came together to discuss how to protect marine life and stop damage to the environment.

**Impact** – This meeting is important because it shows Kenya is taking strong steps to deal with coastal erosion caused by climate change. By bringing experts and leaders together, the country hopes to find real solutions that can help communities affected by the rising waters.

**What's next step** – The ideas and plans from the conference will guide future actions to protect Kenya’s coastline. Communities, scientists, and policymakers will work together to put these solutions into practice and monitor their success.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Kenya is actively working with experts and leaders to protect its coast from the growing effects of climate change and rising sea levels.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Kenya réunit des experts pour protéger sa côte des dégâts du changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La côte kényane le long de l'océan Indien fait face depuis dix ans à des problèmes sérieux comme la baisse des poissons, la pollution et l'érosion causée par la montée des eaux.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Récemment, le Kenya a organisé une grande conférence sur la protection des océans. Des milliers de scientifiques, responsables gouvernementaux et donateurs se sont réunis pour discuter de la préservation de la vie marine et de la lutte contre les dégâts environnementaux.

**Impact** – Cette réunion est importante car elle montre que le Kenya prend des mesures fortes pour combattre l'érosion côtière liée au changement climatique. En rassemblant experts et dirigeants, le pays espère trouver des solutions concrètes pour aider les communautés touchées par la montée des eaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les idées et plans issus de la conférence guideront les actions futures pour protéger la côte kényane. Les communautés, scientifiques et décideurs travailleront ensemble pour mettre en œuvre ces solutions et suivre leurs résultats.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Le Kenya travaille activement avec experts et dirigeants pour protéger sa côte des effets croissants du changement climatique et de la montée des eaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Kenia une a expertos para proteger su costa del daño por cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La costa de Kenia en el océano Índico ha enfrentado problemas graves durante los últimos diez años, como la disminución de peces, contaminación y erosión debido al aumento del nivel del mar.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, Kenia organizó una gran conferencia sobre la protección del océano. Miles de científicos, líderes gubernamentales y donantes se reunieron para hablar sobre cómo proteger la vida marina y detener el daño ambiental.

**Impacto** – Esta reunión es importante porque muestra que Kenia está tomando medidas fuertes para enfrentar la erosión costera causada por el cambio climático. Al juntar a expertos y líderes, el país espera encontrar soluciones reales que ayuden a las comunidades afectadas por el aumento del agua.

**Próximo paso** – Las ideas y planes de la conferencia guiarán las acciones futuras para proteger la costa de Kenia. Comunidades, científicos y autoridades trabajarán juntos para poner en práctica estas soluciones y seguir sus resultados.

**Frase clave** – Kenia está trabajando activamente con expertos y líderes para proteger su costa de los crecientes efectos del cambio climático y la subida del nivel del mar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vibrant East African coastal village where stylized waves gently lap against eroding shorelines, with silhouetted fishing boats and nets resting on the sand, while abstract figures made of flowing water and earth intertwine symbolizing the connection between communities and the changing ocean, all rendered in natural, muted blues, sandy browns, and soft greens.
